steps.title: Spot the core trade-off
steps.content: The passage directly contrasts historians' “cold and naked” work with the “mimic charm” of landscape paintings, then reveals why: historians are bound by “literal truth” and “exactness,” methods that conflict with charm and engagement.

steps.title: Apply the constraint to the choices
steps.content:
Choice B matches: the passage says historians' work is "cold and naked, and wholly destitute of the mimic charm produced by landscape painting" because they are bound by "literal truth" and "biographical exactness" — their accuracy comes at the cost of engagement.
  • A — the passage says the defects are "partly remediable" without "violation of literal truth," implying historians should care about being accurate, not that they shouldn't worry if readers think they're embellishing.
  • C — the passage says nothing about historians disagreeing with each other on methods.
  • D — the map/painting comparison is an analogy for the historian/biographer vs. artistic writing, not a claim about owning physical objects.
That matches choice B — B is correct.
